coredata.dc:title: Metallic deep eutectic solvent derived chiral carbon dots functionalized MOF@silica composite: A new and highly efficient column packing material
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenwen
Ke, Ziyi
Liu, Yanjuan
Xiong, Junjie
Chen, Wei
Tang, Sheng
coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) enable advantageous chromatographic separation through tunable porosity, high surface area and tailored surface chemistry. Although MOF@SiO2 composite shows excellent adsorption/separation potential, its performance is limited by insufficient interaction sites. Carbon dots (CDs) feature abundant surface polar groups that significantly strengthen various interactions with analytes. Consequently, integrating CDs into MOF can enhance overall performance through synergistic effects. In this study, iron metal-doped chiral CDs were synthesized using a metallic deep eutectic solvent as a green reaction medium and precursor, and then encapsulated into MOF-808 to create Fe-CD/MOF composite via one-pot synthesis. This work represents the first instance of employing metal-doped chiral CDs to improve the performance of MOF@SiO2 stationary phase. The experimental findings demonstrate that Fe-CD functionalization provides an effective strategy to modulate retention mechanisms and enhance separation performance. The Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 outperforms the MOF-808@SiO2 in separation efficiency, highlighting the critical contribution of Fe-CD in the composite material. The Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column can achieve high-resolution separation of hydrophilic/hydrophobic/amphiphilic compounds and structural/enantiomeric isomers. In conclusion, a novel and viable method was proposed for the utilization of CD/MOF-based composite as an efficient material for liquid chromatographic separation, thus broadening the application range of CDs and MOFs in separation research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic framework
Carbon dot
Metallic deep eutectic solvent
Stationary phase
Chromatography

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A synthetic scheme for the preparation of Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2.
(a) SEM image of Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2; (b) FT-IR spectra; (c) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distribution curves; (d) Thermogravimetric curves.

Chromatographic separation of PAHs (a), SAs (b) and NBs (c) on the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column. Eluent: (a) ACN/H2O = 27/73 (v/v), (b) ACN/H2O = 90/10 (v/v), (c) ACN/buffer (30 mM ammonium acetate) = 60/40 (v/v).
Chromatograms for positional isomers of (a) dinitrobenzene, (b) chloroaniline, (c) bromoaniline and (d) iodoaniline on the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column. Eluent: n-hexane/isopropanol = 95/5 (v/v).
Chiral resolution of (a) 1-(p-tolyl)ethanol and (b) 1-phenylethanol enantiomers on the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column. Eluent: n-hexane/isopropanol = 90/10 (v/v).

(a) Plots of k vs. ACN (%) for PAHs; (b) The fitted curves of ln k vs. φ ACN for PAHs; (c) Plots of k vs. ACN (%) for NBs; (d) The fitted curves of ln k vs. φ water for NBs; (e) Plots of k vs. ACN (%) for SAs; (f) Plots of k vs. salt concentration for NBs; (g) Plots of k vs. pH for NBs; (h) Chromatograms of PAHs on the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column at different temperatures; (i) The van’t Hoff plots for PAHs.

Chromatograms and van’t Hoff plots for NBs (a, b), 1-(p-tolyl)ethanol enantiomers (c, d), dinitrobenzene (e, f), chloroaniline (g, h), bromoaniline (i, j) and iodoaniline (k, l) on the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column.

Chromatograms of dinitrobenzene isomers (a) and 1-(p-tolyl)ethanol enantiomers (c) at different analyte masses on the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column. Effect of analyte masses on the retention time and peak area of dinitrobenzene isomers (b) and 1-(p-tolyl)ethanol enantiomers (d).
(a) Detection of nicotinamide in liquid sports drink. Eluent: ACN/H2O = 80/20 (v/v). (b) The linear regression curve between nicotinamide concentration and peak area.

head.ce:title: Metallic deep eutectic solvent derived chiral carbon dots functionalized MOF@silica composite: A new and highly efficient column packing material

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Medicine, Linyi University, Linyi 276000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Medicine, Linyi University, Linyi 276000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mangshi Customs Comprehensive Technical Service Center, Dehong 678400, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Mangshi Customs Comprehensive Technical Service Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Mangshi Customs Comprehensive Technical Service Center, Dehong 678400, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) enable advantageous chromatographic separation through tunable porosity, high surface area and tailored surface chemistry. Although MOF@SiO2 composite shows excellent adsorption/separation potential, its performance is limited by insufficient interaction sites. Carbon dots (CDs) feature abundant surface polar groups that significantly strengthen various interactions with analytes. Consequently, integrating CDs into MOF can enhance overall performance through synergistic effects. In this study, iron metal-doped chiral CDs were synthesized using a metallic deep eutectic solvent as a green reaction medium and precursor, and then encapsulated into MOF-808 to create Fe-CD/MOF composite via one-pot synthesis. This work represents the first instance of employing metal-doped chiral CDs to improve the performance of MOF@SiO2 stationary phase. The experimental findings demonstrate that Fe-CD functionalization provides an effective strategy to modulate retention mechanisms and enhance separation performance. The Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 outperforms the MOF-808@SiO2 in separation efficiency, highlighting the critical contribution of Fe-CD in the composite material. The Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column can achieve high-resolution separation of hydrophilic/hydrophobic/amphiphilic compounds and structural/enantiomeric isomers. In conclusion, a novel and viable method was proposed for the utilization of CD/MOF-based composite as an efficient material for liquid chromatographic separation, thus broadening the application range of CDs and MOFs in separation research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), as a category of inorganic-organic hybrid materials, are utilized in biomedicine [1], gas storage and separation [2], water treatment [3], electrochemical sensing [4], catalysis [5,6] and optics [7–9], owing to their tunable pore dimensions, diverse topologies and high porosity. The functionality of MOF materials is not solely contingent upon their pore size, but also upon the groups on the pore surface and unsaturated metal sites, enabling molecule-specific recognition for enhanced chromatographic separations [10,11]. MOF-808 is a zirconium-based MOF constructed from 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC) linkers and inorganic secondary building units [12]. MOF-808 features tetrahedral and adamantane cages, forming an overall diamond-like network with unique topology and high porosity. MOF-808 demonstrates considerable promise in adsorption and separation, attributable to its exceptional structural stability, which stems from the strong Lewis acidity of Zr4+ and the robustness of Zr-O bonds. Direct use of MOF crystals with irregular morphology and uneven size as chromatographic stationary phases presents significant challenges, including high backpressure, peak broadening and low separation efficiency [13]. To address these limitations, silica microsphere, as the preferred material for HPLC separation [14–18], can be integrated with MOF, forming MOF@SiO2 composites [19]. Nevertheless, the utilization of MOF@SiO2 composites as stationary phases is still constrained by the restricted interaction sites. At present, a prevalent methodology entails enhancing the comprehensive performance of MOF-based composites through the incorporation of additional functional materials [20,21], thereby eliciting a synergistic effect.

Carbon dots (CDs) possess multiple intrinsic properties that are conducive to improving chromatographic separation efficiency [22]. The abundant polar functional groups on the surface of CD enables it to have the potential to significantly enhance the hydrogen bonding interactions with the analytes. Therefore, CDs can provide abundant interaction sites to effectively enhance the separation efficiency [23,24]. Furthermore, CDs can effectively mitigate the tailing phenomenon that arises from the intense interactions between the large π-conjugated system and specific analytes [22]. Despite these advantages, there is still substantial potential for enhancing the adsorption capacity of CDs, and this can be achieved through multiple methods. Firstly, doping metal ion can markedly augment the acidic surface functional groups of CDs [25], thereby markedly enhancing the adsorption capacity for alkaline substances. Moreover, metal doping can alter the charge distribution on the surface of the CDs, thereby markedly enhancing the electrostatic attraction to the oppositely charged analytes [26]. Secondly, the inherent properties of CDs can be utilized to introduce chiral monomers to create exceptional chiral selectivity. This strategy endows the CDs with considerable potential for chiral recognition and separation. Presently, the majority of methodologies employed for the synthesis of CDs are conducted in organic solvents, which may result in the production of volatile, flammable and toxic compounds. Deep eutectic solvent (DES) is a low-cost and environmentally friendly analog of ionic liquid [27]. It is acknowledged that few studies have reported the synthesis of CDs by employing green DES. Particularly, no prior studies have reported the preparation of CDs using DES serving dual roles as both reaction precursor and solvent for HPLC applications. The designable properties of DES enable the provision of sufficient and targeted active sites by adjusting the type and molar ratio of precursors during the preparation of CDs [28,29].

In this work, a metallic DES (MDES) was obtained through a green synthesis method by employing DL-malic acid/glycerol as the hydrogen bond acceptor/donor and FeCl3·6H2O as the metal source [30]. MDES serves dual functions in the synthesis of Fe-CDs, acting as both the solvent and reaction precursor. DL-malic acid, utilized as a precursor in fabricating CDs, provides a chiral microenvironment for chiral separation. Doping with iron ions significantly increases the acidic groups on the surface of CDs, thereby improving the separation performance. Subsequently, Fe-CDs were encapsulated in the pores of porous MOF-808 as guests [31], thus obtaining Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2. The obtained composite material ingeniously integrates the high specific surface area and structural stability of MOF-808 with the abundant surface functional groups and metal doping characteristics of CDs, synergistically enhancing hydrophilicity, hydrogen bonding interactions and chiral recognition capabilities, thereby overcoming the limitations of the conventional stationary phases that rely on restricted separation mechanisms. The separation capability of the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 was evaluated using a diverse array of analytes, including positional/enantiomeric isomers, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), sulfonamides (SAs) and nucleosides (NBs). The retention mechanisms and influencing factors were also investigated. In addition, the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column was used to quantitatively detect the content of nicotinamide in liquid sports drink and the qualitative detection of PAHs in lake water, respectively.

Firstly, 13.65 g of DL-malic acid, 30 mL of glycerol and 13.75 g of FeCl3·6H2O were combined and reacted at 80 °C for 30 min, yielding a uniform and transparent liquid phase (MDES). After that, 20 mL of MDES was thoroughly mixed with 40 mL of water using ultrasonication. The above mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the solution was subjected to filtration and loaded into a dialysis bag (MW: 200 Da) for 24 h. Subsequently, the solution was lyophilized to obtain Fe-CDs. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis validates the coexistence of C, O, and Fe (Fig. S1), serving as conclusive evidence for the successful synthesis of Fe-CDs [32,33]. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging analysis reveals that the Fe-CDs exhibit an average particle size of 5.43 nm (Fig. S2).

The synthesis of SiO2-NH2 followed a previously reported procedure [34,35]. A mixture containing 3 g of SiO2-NH2 and 1 g of ZrOCl2·8H2O was dispersed in 60 mL of DMF/formic acid (1/1, v/v). This mixture was refluxed with stirring at 115 °C for 3 h under a nitrogen atmosphere. Subsequently, 0.22 g of H3BTC and 0.25 g of Fe-CDs were introduced, and the reaction proceeded at 115 °C for 72 h. After the reaction was completed, the resulting product was washed with DMF and methanol, and then vacuum-dried to finally obtain the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 (Fig. 1 ). The procedure for column packing followed a previously described method with the modification that the slurry solvent was n-hexane/isopropanol (25/5, v/v) [20].

It can be observed from Fig. 2 a that the Fe-CD/MOF material was successfully modified on the silica surface. The surface of the silica microspheres was attached with flakes and octahedral materials, and it is hypothesized that the original octahedral structure of MOF-808 may have been altered due to the presence of CDs [36]. Infrared absorption peaks at 758 and 649 cm−1 in Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 and MOF-808 belong to the Zr-O bond (Fig. 2b and S3) [37,38], and the peak at 1443 cm−1 is attributed to the benzene in H3BTC of MOF-808, proving that the combination of MOF-808 and silica microspheres is successful. Besides, the peaks at 1657 and 1621 cm−1 correspond to the C=O group in MOF-808 and Fe-CDs. The peak at 3423 cm−1 in Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 is attributed to the O–H stretching vibration, whereas the corresponding peak in Fe-CDs exhibits a characteristic red shift to 3373 cm−1. This phenomenon is primarily due to the formation of significant intermolecular hydrogen bonds during the synthesis of MDES from DL-malic acid and glycerol. The peak at 1104 cm−1 in Fe-CDs is ascribed to C-O stretching vibration. The XRD pattern of the synthesized MOF-808 shows excellent consistency with simulations (Fig. S4), confirming high crystallinity. As for Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2, reduced peak intensities indicate modified crystallinity while preserving framework integrity. MOF-808 exhibits a high surface area of 1562.21 m2 g−1 (Table S1, Fig. S5). This yields a substantially higher specific surface area for Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 compared to SiO2-NH2. However, the surface modification of Fe-CD/MOF reduces the pore size and pore volume of SiO2-NH2 (Table S1, Fig. 2c), which proves that Fe-CDs have been successfully embedded in the pores of MOF-808. In addition, elemental analysis shows a 3.19 % increase in carbon content for Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 compared to SiO2-NH2 (Table S2). Thermogravimetric analysis reveals a 13.35 % weight loss for the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2, exceeding the 7.35 % loss observed for SiO2-NH2 (Fig. 2d). Collectively, these characterization results unequivocally demonstrate the successful fabrication of the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2.

PAHs are a class of carcinogens that present a considerable hazard to human health and are not readily biodegradable in the environment. PAHs can accumulate in soil, water and living organisms, leading to persistent detrimental impacts on ecosystems and human health. Thus, detecting and separating PAHs is of vital importance. Four PAHs were selected for the performance evaluation of Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2. Benzene, fluorene, fluoranthene and benz[a]anthracene were effectively separated on the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column (Fig. 3 a). The retention time of the PAHs is observed to increase concomitantly with the rising Log P values (Table S3), suggesting that hydrophobic interactions significantly contribute to the separation mechanism. In addition, the π-conjugated groups present in the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 can engage in π-π stacking interactions with the PAHs. Therefore, as the number of π-electrons of benzene, fluorene, fluoranthene, and benz[a]anthracene increases, the strength of π-π interactions and the analyte retention accordingly increase. In conclusion, it is demonstrated that the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column has certain aromatic selectivity and hydrophobic separation performance.

SAs represent a widely utilized class of antimicrobial drugs, commonly employed for combating bacterial infections. The testing of SAs drugs serves to guarantee their efficacy and safety. As illustrated in Fig. 3b, the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column demonstrates good capability to detect and separate four SAs within 6 min. The elution order of sulfanilamide, sulfamethazine and sulfalen is found to be positively correlated with the number of hydrogen bond acceptor/donor, indicating that hydrogen-bonding interactions are the primary driving force in the separation of these three SAs. In contrast, sulfathiazole is more susceptible to hydrophilic partition based on the Log P value (Table S4), resulting in a more pronounced retention than sulfalen. Consequently, the separation of SAs is dictated by the synergistic interplay of multiple mechanisms.

The chromatographic performance comparison reveals distinct differences in separation efficiency and retention characteristics between the MOF-808@SiO2 and the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2. The MOF-808@SiO2 exhibits inadequate chromatographic resolution for the SAs, failing to achieve baseline separation of sulfanilamide, sulfamethazine and sulfalen, while simultaneously displaying suboptimal retention capability (Fig. S6). This performance limitation likely stems from insufficient specific interactions between the bare MOF-808 and the target analytes. In comparison, the 0.5-Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 demonstrates remarkably improved retention capacity, yet still fails to fully resolve sulfanilamide and sulfamethazine.Notably, when the Fe-CD content in the composite is further increased to yield the optimized Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2, both the overall retention capacity and separation efficiency are further improved, enabling the ideal separation of SAs (Fig. 3b). This pronounced improvement suggests that the incorporation of Fe-CD introduces additional interaction sites and modulates the hydrophilicity of the composite material. These findings convincingly confirm that the Fe-CD functionalization serves as an effective strategy for tailoring retention mechanism and separation performance. Significantly, this is achieved through enhanced multiple interactions and improved hydrophilic characteristics of the composite material.

Nucleoside drugs have excellent antiviral and anti-tumor effects, so they occupy a pivotal position in the medical field. The Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column displays excellent separation selectivity and separation efficiency for NBs, and effective separation of three NBs including uracil, adenine and hypoxanthine was achieved (Fig. 3c). The elution sequence of uracil and adenine negatively correlates with their Log P values (Table S5), suggesting that their separation may be primarily driven by hydrophilicity. However, hypoxanthine exhibits the strongest retention. This is likely because the oxygen atom possesses greater electronegativity than the nitrogen atom, consequently resulting in forming more robust hydrogen-bonding interactions with Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2. It is demonstrated that the modification of Fe-CDs and MOF-808 significantly improves the hydrophilic selectivity and separation performance for polar compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of positional and enantiomeric isomers
ce:section.ce:para: Resolution of chiral enantiomers and aromatic positional isomers is particularly challenging due to their subtle structural differences. HPLC is a widely used technology in the separation of positional and enantiomeric isomers [39]. Different chromatographic column packings can be designed to identify and separate these chiral substances and positional isomers accurately.

Disubstituted benzenes are important raw materials for organic synthesis, which are mainly used as important intermediates for dyes, pesticides and drug synthesis, etc. Given their environmental risks, the effective separation of disubstituted benzene isomers is crucial for both ecological protection and industrial applications. In this work, the separation capabilities of Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column for the positional isomers were thoroughly evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4 , four types of positional isomers were efficiently separated, with dinitrobenzene eluting in the sequence of p-, m-, o-, as well as chloroaniline, bromoaniline and iodoaniline eluting in the sequence of o-, m-, p-. The resolution of positional isomers results from the synergistic effect of multiple interacting forces, molecular structure and polarity. It can be observed that the positional isomers of bromoaniline, chloroaniline and iodoaniline demonstrate enhanced chromatographic retention with increasing L/W ratio, following the sequence of o- < m- < p- (Fig. S7). However, the elution sequence of dinitrobenzene isomers (p- < m- < o) is consistent with the order of dipole moment, but inversely correlates with L/W ratio. This indicates that L/W ratio and dipole–dipole interactions are crucial factors in determining the retention of positional isomers [40].

In this study, CCDs synthesized from DL-malic acid as a precursor were incorporated into MOF-808 to provide a chiral microenvironment for chiral separation. Accordingly, 1-(p-tolyl)ethanol and 1-phenylethanol enantiomers were selected as model compounds to evaluate the enantioselective separation performance of Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the two chiral enantiomers of 1-(p-tolyl)ethanol and 1-phenylethanol were successfully separated and identified. The separation mechanism of enantiomers on stationary phases is intricate [41], and a multitude of interactions may occur during chiral recognition, including host–guest, π-π, hydrogen-bonding, complexation and dipole–dipole interactions as well as spatial steric effects [42,43]. The “three-point theory” of chiral separation suggests that at least three intermolecular forces need to act simultaneously for achieving chiral separation [44]. Firstly, the analytes can enter the MOF cavity to engage in a host–guest interaction with the embedded CCDs, which is a crucial aspect of chiral separation [45]. Secondly, the –OH group presents in the analytes has the potential to interact with the polar groups of CCDs and MOF through hydrogen bonding, thereby separating the enantiomers of these alcohols [46]. Furthermore, the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 is rich in benzene ring structures, which facilitate π-π interactions and steric hindrance effects with enantiomers containing benzene rings. Collectively, the separation of enantiomers is accomplished by a combination of interaction forces.

In reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC), the retention factor (k) of hydrophobic compounds decreases as the proportion of organic solvent in the eluent increases. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, the impact of ACN content on the retention behavior of four PAHs was examined. The k of benzene, fluorene, fluoranthene, and benz[a]anthracene exhibits disparate degrees of decline with the augmentation of ACN content from 30 % to 50 %. This phenomenon is characteristic of RPLC retention. Eq. (1) illustrates the hydrophobic retention mechanism (Supplementary Material) [47]. Fig. 6b and Table S6 demonstrate a strong correlation (R 2 > 0.999) between the ln k and the volume fraction of ACN (φ ACN), indicating that hydrophobic interactions are dominant factors in the separation of PAHs on the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column.

In hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC), the k of polar compounds exhibits a positive correlation with the percentage of organic modifier. Higher organic phase content promotes hydrophilic analyte partitioning into the aqueous layer on the stationary phase surface as well as expands this aqueous layer, synergistically enhancing HILIC retention [48]. As shown in Fig. 6c, the influence of ACN content on the retention behavior of three NBs was examined. The k of uracil, adenine and hypoxanthine is observed to incrementally rise with an increase in ACN content, demonstrating a characteristic hydrophilic retention behavior. The separation of hydrophilic analytes on HILIC stationary phase typically relies on partitioning and adsorption mechanisms. The correlation between analyte retention and the volumetric fraction of water was explored using Eq. (2) (Supplementary Material) [49]. As can be seen from Fig. 6d, good fitting curves for uracil, adenine, and hypoxanthine were obtained with R 2 values exceeding 0.9998 (Table S7). The fitting results show that the hydrophilic separation is a synergistic effect of adsorption and distribution.

Furthermore, the impact of varying ACN concentration on the retention mechanism of the amphiphilic SAs on Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column was examined. The k of SAs displays a “U-shaped” dependence on ACN content, decreasing initially and then increasing at higher organic fractions (Fig. 6e). The RPLC-HILIC transition occurs at 80 % ACN for sulfanilamide/sulfalen and 70 % ACN for sulfamethazine/sulfathiazole. These findings confirm mixed-mode retention mechanisms on the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 stationary phase.

To further understand the effect of mobile phase composition on separation in normal phase liquid chromatography (NPLC), positional isomers (Fig. S8) and enantiomeric isomers (Fig. S9) were analyzed using eluents with different ratios of n-hexane/isopropanol (IPA). Increasing IPA content enhances eluent polarity, resulting in a more potent elution effect, which reduces the analyte retention. This exhibits a typical retention mechanism of NPLC. The primary cause of this phenomenon likely stems from the addition of IPA may attenuate the hydrogen bonding interactions between the analytes and stationary phase. Specifically, IPA occupies hydrogen bonding sites on the stationary phase, thereby leading to a decrease in analyte retention [50]. An increase in IPA content results in a more rapid separation, although this also results in a diminished degree of separation. To achieve an equilibrium between the separation effect and analysis speed, it is essential to select an appropriate ratio of mobile phase.

Salt concentration can modulate analyte retention by altering hydrophilic partitioning and hydrogen bonding/electrostatic interactions [51]. The retention capacity of the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column for uracil, adenine and hypoxanthine all exhibits a trend of initially decreasing and subsequently increasing with the rise in buffer salt concentration (Fig. 6f). The k of NBs decreases significantly as the salt concentration increases from 10 mM to 40 mM, probably due to the fact that increasing buffer salt concentration shields against electrostatic interactions and weakens analyte retention. However, a slight increase in the k of NBs can be observed when the buffer salt concentration is further raised to 50 mM. This likely stems from strengthened hydrogen-bonding interactions induced by the salting-out effect of concentrated mobile-phase ions [52]. In addition, higher salt concentrations promote buffer salt migration into the water-enriched layer, resulting in increased layer thickness and enhanced the retention of polar analytes. Thus, hydrogen bonding interactions and hydrophilic partitioning replace electrostatic shielding as the major influencing factors at higher buffer salt concentrations (50 mM). These results imply that the retention behavior of uracil, adenine and hypoxanthine is collectively governed by multiple interactions.

Changes in mobile phase pH may alter the charged state of Fe-CD/MOF and polar analytes, thereby affecting the electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding, resulting in altered retention behavior. As depicted in Fig. 6g, the k of uracil, adenine and hypoxanthine increases as pH increases. Higher pH reduces protonation of both the polar analytes and the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2, thereby weakening electrostatic repulsion and leading to longer retention time.

Column temperature is a critical parameter governing analyte retention, primarily through its effects on mobile phase viscosity and solute diffusion coefficients. Thermodynamic studies were conducted using model analytes for each separation mode: PAHs for RPLC (Fig. 6h and 6i), NBs for HILIC (Fig. 7 a and 7b), and 1-(p-tolyl)ethanol enantiomers (Fig. 7c and 7d) along with positional isomers for NPLC (Fig. 7e-7l). The correlation between the k and column temperature is characterized using the van’t Hoff equation (Eq. (3), Supplementary Material) [53].

In different modes, a strong linear correlation can be observed for all analytes (Table S8), indicating that the retention mechanism remains consistent across varying column temperatures. The retention of the analytes on the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column decreases as the temperature increases. This phenomenon is likely due to the decrease in eluent viscosity at higher temperatures, thereby facilitating a faster elution of the analytes. Furthermore, the negative ΔH values confirm that the analyte transfer from the mobile phase to the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 is an exothermic process, and low temperatures are more favorable for analyte retention. This thermodynamic profile clearly indicates an enthalpy-driven retention mechanism [54].

The loading capacity of chromatographic column represents a critical operational parameter, where exceeding this limit leads to compromised separation performance. To investigate the effect of injection mass on the separation efficiency, a series of injections were performed using different masses (1, 2, 5, 10, 15, 20 μg) of dinitrobenzene isomers (Fig. 8 a) and 1-(p-tolyl)ethanol enantiomers (Fig. 8c). Fig. 8b and 8d illustrate a linear correlation between the injection mass and peak area. The peak area shows linear proportionality to the injection mass, while retention time remains virtually constant. When the injection mass reaches 20 μg, a slight decline in resolution can be noted. This implies that the column loading capacity is at least 20 μg. Consequently, the injection mass within a certain range has a negligible impact on the separation ability of the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column.

It is essential that the developed chromatographic column should feature superior robustness. The chromatographic behavior of dinitrobenzene isomers was used to characterize the operational reliability of the Fe-CDs/MOF-808@SiO2 column. Fig. S10a demonstrates the intra-day repeatability through ten consecutive separations, while Fig. S10b shows inter-day stability over three days with triplicate daily runs. Repeated injections exhibit stable retention time and resolution, indicating robust column performance. The low relative standard deviation (RSD) values of 0.11–0.68 % (intra-day) and 0.48–0.69 % (inter-day) confirm remarkable operational stability of the Fe-CDs/MOF-808@SiO2 column (Fig. S11).

Nicotinamide, also designated as vitamin B3, is implicated in lipid metabolism, oxidative processes of tissue respiration and anaerobic breakdown of sugars within the body. The addition of nicotinamide to liquid sports drinks is a prevalent practice, as it allows for adaptation to the physiological characteristics of individuals engaged in physical activity. However, most commercial products only declare approximate ranges of nicotinamide content, making it challenging to evaluate its actual nutritional efficacy. Therefore, accurate quantification of nicotinamide in sports beverages carries significant practical implications. In this study, the practical utility of Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column was validated by the precise determination of the nicotinamide content in liquid sports drink (Fig. 9 a). A calibration curve was established by analyzing nicotinamide standard solutions across different concentrations (0.1–1000 μg mL−1), demonstrating excellent linearity (R 2 = 0.9999) between the peak area and the concentration (Fig. 9b and Table S9). The limits of detection and quantification are 0.023 and 0.078 μg mL−1, respectively. Moreover, method accuracy was verified through spike-recovery experiments at three concentration levels (50, 100 and 500 μg mL−1). Six replicate analyses at each level demonstrate excellent precision (RSD: 0.08–0.14 %) and quantitative recovery (98.91–100.82 %), confirming the reliability of the developed method (Table S10). Eventually, the nicotinamide content in the actual liquid sports drink was determined to be 15.25 μg mL−1 by using the validated method.

PAHs, classified as persistent organic pollutants, exhibit environmental persistence and bioaccumulation potential. Given their ubiquitous presence in aquatic systems and propensity for food-chain biomagnification, monitoring of PAHs in water bodies is critical for ecological and public health protection. As demonstrated in Fig. S12, the analysis of PAHs in lake water exhibits negligible matrix interference, confirming the reliability of the analytical method for complex environmental samples. The aforementioned findings suggest that the Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column holds promising potential for dual applications in food and environmental analysis.

In this study, Fe-CD and MOF-808 were selected as innovative modifiers of silica microspheres for the first time, resulting in the preparation of a new HPLC stationary phase. The combination of Fe-CD and MOF-808 exhibits a pronounced synergistic effect, whereby the advantages of the two materials are integrated to produce a more effective and robust material. The DL-malic acid acts as a reaction precursor for Fe-CD, enabling enantiomer separation while introducing additional interaction sites. The tailored functionalization of Fe-CD/MOF creates multiple interaction modes, facilitating versatile chromatographic separations. The Fe-CD/MOF@SiO2 column demonstrates exceptional chromatographic performance, exhibiting strong potential for food and environmental monitoring applications. This study confirms metal-doped CD/MOF composites as viable HPLC separation materials with significant potential for advancing liquid chromatography. Nevertheless, current column efficiency remains suboptimal, warranting further development of enhanced CD/MOF composites for HPLC stationary phases.

Wenwen Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ziyi Ke: Investigation, Validation. Yanjuan Liu: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Junjie Xiong: Resources. Wei Chen: Supervision. Sheng Tang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21906124, 32302202), Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (No. 2017CFB220) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2023MH278).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stationary and mobile phases in hydrophilic interaction chromatography: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Jandera, Stationary and mobile phases in hydrophilic interaction chromatography: a review, Anal. Chim. Acta 692 (2011) 1-25. http://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2011.02.047.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Performance evaluation of 2-undecylimidazole/propyl methacrylate bifunctional silica gel for mixed-mode reversed-phase/anion-exchange chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.F. Hu, Y.H. Jiang, P.P. Cao, Y.J. Liu, Y.F. Zhang, W. Chen, S. Tang, Performance evaluation of 2-undecylimidazole/propyl methacrylate bifunctional silica gel for mixed-mode reversed-phase/anion-exchange chromatography, Microchem. J. 191 (2023) 108768. http://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108768.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graft copolymerization of anion and cation onto silica and application in mixed-mode of reversed phase/hydrophilic interaction/ion exchange chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, H.J. Peng, Z.L. Zhang, J.J. Wu, J.Y. Yu, H.L. Zeng, H.Q. Yang, G.M. Zhou, J.D. Peng, Graft copolymerization of anion and cation onto silica and application in mixed-mode of reversed phase/hydrophilic interaction/ion exchange chromatography, Talanta 266 (2024) 125055. http://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.125055.
